misconceptionSummary: Students add frequencies from different levels of the tree, double-counting groups.

lines.text: Tree: total 100, boys 60, girls 40; boys-pass 35, boys-fail 25
Added the boys node AND both its children — double-counted

steps.narration: The student added the boys node and its two children. But the children are already contained within the boys node — adding them again counts the boys twice. Each person appears in exactly one leaf.

visuals.ruleText: Read from ONE level of the tree for any given question — never mix nodes from different levels
visuals.subText: A parent node already includes all its children — do not add children to it

steps.narration: A parent node is the sum of its children — they are not separate counts to be added together. To find the total in a category, read from one level only: either the parent node or the sum of its leaves, but not both.

lines.text: How many boys? → read the boys node: 60  ✓
How many passed? → add pass leaves: 35 + 28 = 63  ✓
Never add a node to its own children

steps.narration: For the total boys, simply read sixty from the boys node. For total passes, add the pass leaves from each gender branch. Pick the right level — never mix.

visuals.expression: Boys = 60 (read the node — do not add its children)
visuals.label: One level only — parent already contains its children

steps.narration: Sixty boys. The node already contains the information — its children are breakdowns of that sixty, not additions to it.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: adding 60 + 35 + 25 = 120 for 'number of boys'. The node 60 already includes 35 and 25 — adding them again doubles the count. Read from one level only.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: A parent node already includes all its children. Never add a node to its own children — that double-counts everyone in the group.
